articles.headline: India Unveils Phase II of National Green Hydrogen Mission, Targets Global Leadership
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) launched Phase II of the National Green Hydrogen Mission (NGHM), focusing on scaling up production and establishing export hubs.
• This phase emphasizes R&D in electrolysis technologies, development of green hydrogen derivatives (e.g., green ammonia), and creation of a robust supply chain.
• Financial incentives and viability gap funding for early-stage projects are key components, alongside skill development programs for a green hydrogen workforce.
• The mission aims to reduce dependence on fossil fuel imports, create significant employment opportunities, and position India as a global leader in green hydrogen production and export.
• Environmental impact assessments and sustainable water management practices are integrated to ensure ecological balance.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy
Environmental pollution and degradation
Conservation
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
prelimsPoints: National Green Hydrogen Mission (NGHM) launched in 2023.
Phase II focuses on scaling production and export.
Key objectives include reducing fossil fuel imports and creating jobs.
MNRE is the nodal ministry.
Green hydrogen is produced using renewable energy for electrolysis.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of green hydrogen for India's energy security and climate goals.
Analyze the challenges and opportunities in scaling up green hydrogen production and its derivatives.
Evaluate the potential of NGHM Phase II to transform India into a global green hydrogen hub.
Examine the socio-economic and environmental implications of a large-scale green hydrogen economy in India.
articles.imageDescription: Illustration of a green hydrogen plant with wind turbines and solar panels in the background.
articles.trivia: Hydrogen is the most abundant chemical substance in the universe, making up roughly 75% of all baryonic mass.

articles.headline: Supreme Court Upholds Right to Data Privacy in Landmark Ruling on Digital Surveillance
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court delivered a significant judgment reinforcing the fundamental right to data privacy, ruling against indiscriminate digital surveillance by state agencies.
• The verdict emphasized the need for a robust legal framework, proportionality, and judicial oversight for any surveillance activities, even in matters of national security.
• It called for a review of existing surveillance laws and guidelines to align them with constitutional principles, particularly Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty).
• The ruling is expected to have far-reaching implications for government data collection policies, tech companies operating in India, and individual digital rights.
• Experts laud the judgment as a crucial step towards strengthening democratic freedoms and safeguarding citizens' privacy in the digital age.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Fundamental Rights
prelimsPoints: K.S. Puttaswamy judgment (2017) declared Right to Privacy as a fundamental right.
The current ruling further defines limits on state surveillance.
Article 21 of the Indian Constitution guarantees Right to Life and Personal Liberty.
Surveillance laws need to be reviewed for constitutional compliance.
mainsPoints: Analyze the implications of the Supreme Court's ruling on data privacy for national security and individual liberties.
Discuss the challenges in balancing state security interests with citizens' right to privacy in the digital era.
Examine the role of judicial review in upholding fundamental rights against executive overreach.
Suggest legislative and technological measures to create a robust data privacy framework in India.
articles.imageDescription: Abstract image representing digital data and privacy, with a gavel superimposed.
articles.trivia: The first official Data Protection Bill in India was introduced in 2019, following the recommendations of the Justice B.N. Srikrishna Committee.

articles.headline: India-Japan Strategic Dialogue Focuses on Indo-Pacific Stability and Economic Cooperation
articles.explainer:
• The annual India-Japan Strategic Dialogue concluded with a joint statement emphasizing shared commitment to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific region.
• Discussions centered on enhancing defense and security cooperation, including joint exercises and intelligence sharing, amidst evolving regional dynamics.
• Economic partnership was a key agenda, with agreements signed to boost investments in critical infrastructure, renewable energy, and digital technology sectors.
• Both nations reiterated support for multilateralism and cooperation in global forums, addressing challenges like climate change and supply chain resilience.
• The dialogue also explored new avenues for collaboration in emerging technologies and space exploration, solidifying their 'Special Strategic and Global Partnership'.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: India and Japan are members of the QUAD grouping.
The 'Special Strategic and Global Partnership' defines India-Japan ties.
Both nations collaborate on infrastructure projects in third countries.
Annual Strategic Dialogue reviews bilateral relations.

Discuss the various dimensions of India-Japan cooperation, including defense, economy, and emerging technologies.
Analyze how India-Japan collaboration contributes to global governance and addresses common challenges.
Examine the challenges and opportunities in deepening India-Japan ties in the context of evolving geopolitical landscape.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and Japan side-by-side during a diplomatic meeting.
articles.trivia: Japan is one of the largest investors in India, particularly in infrastructure projects like the Mumbai-Ahmedabad High-Speed Rail.

articles.headline: RBI Expands Digital Rupee (CBDC) Pilot Program to Include More Retail Users and Use Cases
articles.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) announced a significant expansion of its Central Bank Digital Currency (CBDC) pilot project, 'e₹-R' (retail digital rupee).
• The expansion aims to onboard a larger number of retail users and small businesses, testing various new use cases beyond basic peer-to-peer and peer-to-merchant transactions.
• Focus areas include offline functionality, programmable payments, and integration with existing UPI infrastructure to enhance interoperability.
• The RBI seeks to gather more comprehensive data on user experience, technological robustness, and economic impact before a full-scale rollout.
• This move signals India's continued commitment to exploring the potential of digital currencies for financial inclusion and efficient payments.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
prelimsPoints: RBI launched wholesale (e₹-W) and retail (e₹-R) CBDC pilots.
CBDC is a legal tender issued by a central bank in digital form.
It aims to complement, not replace, existing payment systems.
UPI (Unified Payments Interface) is a real-time payment system developed by NPCI.
mainsPoints: Discuss the potential benefits and risks associated with the introduction of a Central Bank Digital Currency (CBDC) in India.
Analyze how the expansion of the digital rupee pilot can impact financial inclusion, monetary policy, and the banking sector.

Compare and contrast CBDC with cryptocurrencies and traditional digital payment methods like UPI.
articles.imageDescription: Digital representation of the Indian Rupee symbol with binary code background.
articles.trivia: Globally, over 100 countries are exploring or have launched their own CBDCs, with The Bahamas being one of the first to issue a retail CBDC (the Sand Dollar).

articles.headline: G20 Health Ministers Pledge Enhanced Global Cooperation for Future Pandemic Preparedness
articles.explainer:
• G20 Health Ministers convened to review progress on strengthening global health architecture and committed to bolstering preparedness for future pandemics.
• Key outcomes included pledges for increased funding for vaccine research and development, equitable access to medical countermeasures, and robust surveillance systems.
• Discussions highlighted the importance of strengthening the World Health Organization (WHO) and other multilateral health bodies in coordinating global responses.
• The ministers also addressed the 'One Health' approach, recognizing the interconnectedness of human, animal, and environmental health in preventing zoonotic diseases.
• India emphasized its role as a 'pharmacy of the world' and offered its manufacturing capabilities for global health security.
gsSubTopics: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
prelimsPoints: G20 is an intergovernmental forum comprising 19 countries and the European Union.
WHO is the specialized agency of the United Nations responsible for international public health.
The 'One Health' approach recognizes health of humans, animals, and environment are linked.
India is a major producer of vaccines and generic drugs.

Evaluate the 'One Health' approach as a strategy for preventing future zoonotic outbreaks.
Examine India's contributions and potential in strengthening global health security and pandemic preparedness.
articles.imageDescription: G20 logo with medical symbols in the background.
articles.trivia: The G20 was formed in 1999 in response to the Asian financial crisis, initially as a meeting of finance ministers and central bank governors.

articles.headline: Parliamentary Panel Recommends Comprehensive Electoral Reforms Ahead of State Elections
articles.explainer:
• A bipartisan Parliamentary Standing Committee submitted its report on electoral reforms, proposing significant changes to the Representation of the People Act.
• Recommendations include simultaneous elections (Lok Sabha and State Assemblies), state funding of elections, and stricter regulations on political donations.
• The panel also suggested measures to curb the criminalization of politics, enhance the transparency of electoral bonds, and improve voter registration processes.
• Emphasis was placed on leveraging technology for efficient election management and ensuring greater participation of women and marginalized sections.
• The report aims to address long-standing issues of electoral malpractices and strengthen democratic principles in India.
gsSubTopics: Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act.
Appointment to various Constitutional posts, powers, functions and responsibilities of various Constitutional Bodies.
Parliament and State Legislatures—structure, functioning, conduct of business, powers & privileges and issues arising out of these.
prelimsPoints: Representation of the People Act, 1950 and 1951 govern elections in India.
Election Commission of India is a constitutional body (Article 324).
Simultaneous elections are often debated for cost reduction and policy continuity.
State funding of elections aims to reduce private influence.
mainsPoints: Discuss the necessity and feasibility of implementing simultaneous elections in India.
Analyze the pros and cons of state funding for elections and its potential impact on Indian democracy.
Examine the challenges in curbing the criminalization of politics and strengthening the integrity of the electoral process.
Evaluate the recommendations of the Parliamentary Panel on electoral reforms and their potential to deepen democratic values.
articles.imageDescription: Parliament building with an electoral ballot box in the foreground.
articles.trivia: The first general elections in independent India were held from October 1951 to February 1952.

articles.headline: ISRO Gears Up for Chandrayaan-4 Mission, Eyes Lunar Sample Return
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) announced accelerated preparations for its ambitious Chandrayaan-4 mission, targeting a lunar sample return.
• This mission aims to collect lunar soil and rock samples from a specific region of the Moon and bring them back to Earth for detailed scientific analysis.
• Chandrayaan-4 will involve a complex multi-module architecture, including an orbiter, lander, ascender, and re-entry module, showcasing advanced Indian space capabilities.
• The mission holds significant scientific promise, potentially shedding new light on the Moon's geological history, composition, and evolution.
• This follows the successful Chandrayaan-3 landing, further cementing India's position in lunar exploration and deep space missions.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
prelimsPoints: Chandrayaan-1 was India's first lunar probe.
Chandrayaan-3 successfully soft-landed on the Moon's South Pole.
ISRO is the primary space agency of India.
Sample return missions are highly complex and provide invaluable scientific data.
mainsPoints: Discuss the scientific significance and technological challenges of lunar sample return missions.
Analyze how India's lunar missions (Chandrayaan series) contribute to global space exploration efforts and national prestige.
Examine the role of ISRO in fostering scientific temper and technological advancements in India.
Evaluate the potential socio-economic benefits derived from advancements in space technology.
articles.imageDescription: Artistic rendering of Chandrayaan-4 modules on the lunar surface, collecting samples.
articles.trivia: The Moon's South Pole is of particular interest to scientists due to the potential presence of water ice in permanently shadowed craters.

articles.headline: Government Fast-Tracks Multi-Modal Connectivity Projects in Northeast India
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Development of North Eastern Region (DoNER) announced accelerated implementation of several multi-modal connectivity projects.
• Projects include new railway lines, expansion of national highways, inland waterways development, and upgradation of regional airports.
• The aim is to enhance economic integration of the Northeast with mainland India and neighboring countries, particularly under the 'Act East' policy.
• These initiatives are expected to boost trade, tourism, and investment, unlocking the region's vast potential and reducing geographical isolation.
• Special focus is on sustainable infrastructure development, respecting the region's ecological fragility and unique cultural heritage.

Development processes and the development industry—the role of NGOs, SHGs, various groups and associations, donors, charities, institutional and other stakeholders.

articles.trivia: The Brahmaputra River, a key inland waterway in the Northeast, is one of the largest rivers in the world by discharge.

articles.headline: New Study Highlights Intensifying Urban Heat Island Effect in Indian Cities
articles.explainer:
• A recent study published in 'Nature Climate Change' indicates a significant increase in the Urban Heat Island (UHI) effect across major Indian cities.
• The research attributes the intensification to rapid urbanization, loss of green spaces, prevalence of heat-absorbing building materials, and anthropogenic heat emissions.
• UHI effect leads to higher temperatures in urban areas compared to surrounding rural regions, exacerbating heatwaves and increasing energy consumption for cooling.
• The study calls for urgent policy interventions, including promoting green infrastructure, cool roofs, urban forestry, and sustainable urban planning practices.
• Implications include increased health risks for vulnerable populations, strain on power grids, and reduced quality of life in densely populated areas.
gsSubTopics: Urbanization, their problems and their remedies.

Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location-changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes.
prelimsPoints: Urban Heat Island (UHI) refers to urban areas being significantly warmer than rural surroundings.
Causes include dark surfaces, lack of vegetation, and human activities.
Impacts include increased energy demand, air pollution, and health issues.
Mitigation strategies involve green roofs, cool pavements, and urban forests.
mainsPoints: Discuss the causes and consequences of the Urban Heat Island (UHI) effect in Indian cities.
Analyze the intersection of urbanization, climate change, and public health in the context of rising urban temperatures.
Suggest comprehensive urban planning and policy measures to mitigate the UHI effect and enhance urban resilience.
Examine the role of local bodies and citizen participation in implementing sustainable urban cooling solutions.
articles.imageDescription: Satellite image showing temperature variations in an urban area, with red hot spots.
articles.trivia: The 'cool roof' technology, which uses reflective materials, can lower indoor temperatures by 2-5°C and reduce air conditioning costs by 15-20%.

articles.headline: Ministry of Education Reviews Progress of National Education Policy 2020 Implementation
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education conducted a high-level review of the implementation progress of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 across states and union territories.
• Key areas of focus included the foundational literacy and numeracy mission, curriculum reforms, teacher training, and integration of vocational education.
• Challenges identified included disparities in infrastructure, digital divide in remote areas, and the need for greater state-level capacity building.
• The review emphasized the importance of a multi-stakeholder approach, involving state governments, educational institutions, and local communities.
• NEP 2020 aims to transform India's education system to meet the demands of the 21st century, promoting holistic, multidisciplinary learning.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: NEP 2020 replaced the 1986 National Policy on Education.
It proposes a 5+3+3+4 curricular structure.
Focuses on early childhood care and education (ECCE).
Aims to achieve 100% Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) in school education by 2030.
mainsPoints: Discuss the transformative potential of the National Education Policy 2020 in addressing the challenges of India's education system.

articles.trivia: The Kothari Commission (1964-66) was instrumental in shaping India's education policy, leading to the first National Policy on Education in 1968.

articles.headline: India-Africa Trade Dialogue Explores New Avenues for Economic Partnership
articles.explainer:
• A high-level India-Africa Trade Dialogue commenced, bringing together policymakers, industry leaders, and business representatives from both regions.
• The dialogue focused on diversifying trade baskets, boosting investment flows, and fostering technology transfer in areas like agriculture, healthcare, and digital services.
• Discussions underscored India's commitment to capacity building and skill development initiatives in African nations, aligning with its 'SAGAR' vision.
• Challenges such as tariff barriers, infrastructure deficits, and market access issues were addressed, with proposals for joint solutions.
• The event aims to strengthen South-South cooperation and build a resilient, mutually beneficial economic partnership.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: India is Africa's fourth-largest trading partner.

AfCFTA is a free trade area encompassing most of Africa.
India extends Lines of Credit (LoC) to African countries for development projects.

articles.trivia: India's first major engagement with Africa was through the Non-Aligned Movement (NAM) during the Cold War era.

articles.headline: Government Intensifies Efforts to Promote Millets Cultivation and Consumption
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare launched new initiatives to further promote the cultivation and consumption of millets, declared as 'Shree Anna'.
• Efforts include increased Minimum Support Price (MSP) for millets, research into high-yielding and climate-resilient varieties, and awareness campaigns.
• The government aims to position millets as a 'superfood' due to their nutritional benefits and low water footprint, aligning with sustainable agriculture goals.
• Support is being provided to farmers for value addition, processing, and market linkages to enhance their income from millet farming.
• This push comes in the wake of the International Year of Millets (2023) and India's leadership in promoting these ancient grains globally.

Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
prelimsPoints: 2023 was declared as the International Year of Millets by the UN, at India's proposal.
Millets are often called 'nutri-cereals'.
Examples of millets include Jowar, Bajra, Ragi.
They are drought-resistant and require less water than rice or wheat.
mainsPoints: Discuss the role of millets in enhancing India's food and nutritional security, especially in the context of climate change.

Evaluate the potential of millets to contribute to global food security and sustainable diets.
articles.imageDescription: Various types of millets grains displayed in bowls.
articles.trivia: Millets were among the first crops domesticated by humans, with evidence of their cultivation dating back over 7,000 years.

articles.headline: NITI Aayog Releases Report on Impact Assessment of Gender Budgeting in India
articles.explainer:
• NITI Aayog published a comprehensive report assessing the effectiveness and impact of Gender Budgeting (GB) initiatives across various ministries and states.
• The report highlights achievements in increasing allocation for women-centric schemes but also points out challenges in tracking outcomes and ensuring gender-sensitive implementation.
• It recommends strengthening institutional mechanisms, improving data collection for gender-disaggregated statistics, and enhancing capacity building for GB practitioners.
• The assessment underscores the need to move beyond mere financial allocations to focus on actual empowerment and equity outcomes for women.
• Gender Budgeting, initiated in India in 2005-06, aims to analyze the budget from a gender perspective to identify how government policies impact women and men differently.
gsSubTopics: Welfare schemes for vulnerable sections of the population by the Centre and States and the performance of these schemes; mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.

prelimsPoints: Gender Budgeting (GB) was introduced in India in 2005-06.
It is a tool to analyze government budgets from a gender perspective.
NITI Aayog is a policy think tank of the Government of India.
GB aims to reduce gender disparities and promote women's empowerment.
mainsPoints: Discuss the concept of Gender Budgeting and its significance as a tool for achieving gender equality and women's empowerment.

Evaluate how Gender Budgeting can be integrated more effectively into policy formulation and implementation across sectors.
articles.imageDescription: Infographic showing a budget pie chart with gender symbols.
articles.trivia: Australia was one of the first countries to introduce gender budgeting in the mid-1980s.

articles.headline: India Strengthens Cybersecurity Framework for Critical Infrastructure
articles.explainer:
• The National Critical Information Infrastructure Protection Centre (NCIIPC) unveiled an updated cybersecurity framework for critical sectors.
• The new guidelines mandate enhanced threat detection systems, regular security audits, and strict data protection protocols for organizations managing essential services.
• Focus areas include energy grids, financial systems, transportation networks, and communication infrastructure, which are vulnerable to cyber-attacks.
• The move comes amidst rising global cyber threats and aims to safeguard national security and economic stability.
• Collaboration between government agencies, private sector entities, and international partners is emphasized for a resilient cyber ecosystem.
gsSubTopics: Challenges to internal security through communication networks, role of media and social networking sites in internal security challenges, basics of cyber security; money-laundering and its prevention.
Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.

prelimsPoints: NCIIPC is an organization under the National Technical Research Organisation (NTRO).
Critical Information Infrastructure (CII) is assets vital to national security and economy.
CERT-In (Indian Computer Emergency Response Team) is the national agency for cyber security incidents.
Cybersecurity is crucial for digital economy and national defense.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolving nature of cyber threats and their implications for India's critical infrastructure and national security.

Examine the challenges in securing critical information infrastructure from sophisticated cyber-attacks.

articles.trivia: The first computer virus, 'Elk Cloner', appeared in 1982, affecting Apple II systems.

articles.headline: National Plan for Wetland Conservation Launches with Focus on Biodiversity and Livelihoods
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) unveiled a comprehensive National Plan for Wetland Conservation and Management.
• The plan emphasizes protection of wetlands as critical ecosystems, crucial for biodiversity, water purification, and climate regulation.
• It integrates local community participation in conservation efforts, promoting sustainable livelihoods dependent on wetlands.
• Key strategies include mapping and inventory of wetlands, restoration of degraded sites, and strict regulation of activities impacting wetland health.
• The initiative aligns with India's commitments under the Ramsar Convention and aims to enhance ecological security and climate resilience.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Biodiversity and its conservation.
prelimsPoints: India is a signatory to the Ramsar Convention on Wetlands.
Ramsar sites are wetlands of international importance.
Wetlands provide various ecosystem services like flood control and carbon sequestration.
MoEFCC is the nodal ministry for environmental protection in India.

articles.headline: Start-up India Seed Fund Scheme Shows Promising Impact on Emerging Ventures
articles.explainer:
• The Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT) released an impact report on the Start-up India Seed Fund Scheme (SISFS).
• The report indicates a significant boost to early-stage startups, enabling them to develop prototypes, conduct market entry, and commercialize products.
• SISFS has particularly benefited startups in Tier 2 and Tier 3 cities, fostering innovation and entrepreneurship beyond major metropolitan areas.
• Challenges remain in scaling up funding for later stages and ensuring mentorship for diverse sectors.
• The scheme, launched in 2021, aims to provide financial assistance to eligible startups for proof of concept, prototype development, product trials, market entry, and commercialization.

articles.headline: Gita Jayanti Celebrated Across India, Emphasizing Ethical Governance
articles.explainer:
• Gita Jayanti, marking the day Lord Krishna delivered the Bhagavad Gita to Arjuna, was celebrated with spiritual fervor nationwide.
• Various events, including discourses, cultural programs, and recitations, highlighted the Gita's timeless wisdom on duty, righteousness, and selfless action.
• Scholars and public figures emphasized the Gita's relevance in contemporary times, particularly its teachings on ethical leadership and governance.
• The philosophical principles of 'Karma Yoga' and 'Dharma' were discussed as guiding tenets for individuals and society.
• The celebrations underscored India's rich cultural heritage and the enduring influence of ancient texts on modern values.

articles.headline: Indian Antarctic Expedition Concludes, Yields Critical Climate Change Data
articles.explainer:
• The latest Indian scientific expedition to Antarctica successfully concluded, with researchers returning with vital data on climate change.
• Studies focused on glacial melt rates, sea-level rise, atmospheric composition, and Antarctic biodiversity, contributing to global climate models.
• The expedition utilized India's permanent research stations, 'Bharati' and 'Maitri', for long-term observations and sample collection.
• The findings are expected to enhance understanding of polar science and the impacts of global warming on the Indian subcontinent's weather patterns.
• India reiterated its commitment to peaceful scientific research in Antarctica under the Antarctic Treaty System.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
prelimsPoints: India has three permanent research base stations in Antarctica: Dakshin Gangotri (now a supply base), Maitri, and Bharati.
The National Centre for Polar and Ocean Research (NCPOR) manages India's polar programs.
The Antarctic Treaty System governs international relations with respect to Antarctica.
Research focuses on climate change, ozone depletion, and earth sciences.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of India's scientific expeditions to Antarctica in the context of global climate change research.
Analyze how data from polar regions contributes to understanding global weather patterns and environmental shifts.

Evaluate the challenges and technological advancements involved in conducting research in extreme polar environments.
articles.imageDescription: Indian research station 'Bharati' in Antarctica amidst icy landscape.
articles.trivia: India launched its first scientific expedition to Antarctica in 1981, marking its entry into polar research.

articles.headline: AYUSH Ministry Promotes Integration of Ayurveda in Mainstream Healthcare
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of AYUSH announced new guidelines and funding to promote the integration of Ayurvedic practices into mainstream healthcare delivery.
• Initiatives include setting up dedicated AYUSH wings in district hospitals, developing standardized treatment protocols, and enhancing research in traditional medicine.

articles.headline: India-Pakistan Hold Constructive Talks on Indus Waters Treaty Issues
articles.explainer:
• Delegations from India and Pakistan held their annual meeting under the Permanent Indus Commission to discuss various aspects of the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT).
• Talks focused on technical issues related to hydroelectric projects on the western rivers and data exchange mechanisms.
• Both sides reiterated their commitment to upholding the provisions of the IWT, a crucial water-sharing agreement between the two nations.
• While no major breakthroughs were announced, officials described the discussions as constructive and aimed at resolving minor disagreements through dialogue.
• The IWT, brokered by the World Bank in 1960, governs the sharing of waters of the Indus River system.

articles.headline: Archaeologists Unearth New Evidence of Ancient Harappan Settlement in Gujarat
articles.explainer:
• A team of Indian archaeologists announced the discovery of a previously unknown Harappan settlement site in the Kutch region of Gujarat.
• Preliminary excavations have revealed pottery, tools, and structural remains, suggesting a thriving urban center dating back to the Mature Harappan period.
• The discovery is expected to provide new insights into the geographical spread, trade networks, and cultural practices of the Indus Valley Civilization.
• Advanced techniques like remote sensing and ground-penetrating radar were utilized in the initial survey, aiding in the identification of the site.
• Further research and extensive excavations are planned to fully understand the significance of this new archaeological find.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the National Green Hydrogen Mission (NGHM), consider the following statements:
1. The mission aims to make India a global hub for the production, utilization, and export of Green Hydrogen.
2. The Ministry of Power is the nodal ministry for the implementation of the NGHM.
3. Viability Gap Funding for pilot projects is a component of the mission.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation:
Statement 1 is correct. A primary objective of NGHM is to position India as a global leader in green hydrogen.
Statement 2 is incorrect. The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) is the nodal ministry for NGHM, not the Ministry of Power.
Statement 3 is correct. Viability Gap Funding is a key component to support early-stage projects and overcome initial cost barriers.

prelimsMocks.question: The recent Supreme Court ruling reinforcing the fundamental right to data privacy is most closely associated with which Article of the Indian Constitution?
prelimsMocks.explanation: The Supreme Court in K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017) declared the Right to Privacy as a fundamental right protected under Article 21 of the Constitution, which guarantees the Right to Life and Personal Liberty. Subsequent rulings, like the one mentioned, further elaborate on its scope.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following statements about the Urban Heat Island (UHI) effect is/are correct?
1. It refers to urban areas experiencing higher temperatures than surrounding rural areas.
2. Loss of green spaces and prevalence of heat-absorbing materials contribute to the UHI effect.
3. The UHI effect can lead to decreased energy consumption for cooling in cities.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation:
Statement 1 is correct. UHI is defined by urban areas being warmer than rural areas.
Statement 2 is correct. Concrete, asphalt, and lack of vegetation absorb and retain more heat.
Statement 3 is incorrect. The UHI effect typically leads to *increased* energy consumption for cooling due to higher temperatures.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding India's National Education Policy (NEP) 2020:
1. It proposes a 10+2 curricular structure for school education.
2. It aims for 100% Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) in school education by 2030.
3. Integration of vocational education from early grades is a key feature.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation:
Statement 1 is incorrect. NEP 2020 proposes a new 5+3+3+4 curricular structure, replacing the 10+2 system.
Statement 2 is correct. Achieving 100% GER in school education by 2030 is a major goal.
Statement 3 is correct. NEP 2020 emphasizes the integration of vocational education from Class 6 onwards.

prelimsMocks.question: The Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) was brokered by which international organization?
prelimsMocks.explanation: The Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) between India and Pakistan was brokered by the World Bank (then the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development) and signed in 1960.

GS1.headline: New Study Highlights Intensifying Urban Heat Island Effect in Indian Cities
GS1.explainer:
• A recent study published in 'Nature Climate Change' indicates a significant increase in the Urban Heat Island (UHI) effect across major Indian cities.
• The research attributes the intensification to rapid urbanization, loss of green spaces, prevalence of heat-absorbing building materials, and anthropogenic heat emissions.
• UHI effect leads to higher temperatures in urban areas compared to surrounding rural regions, exacerbating heatwaves and increasing energy consumption for cooling.
• The study calls for urgent policy interventions, including promoting green infrastructure, cool roofs, urban forestry, and sustainable urban planning practices.
• Implications include increased health risks for vulnerable populations, strain on power grids, and reduced quality of life in densely populated areas.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Upholds Right to Data Privacy in Landmark Ruling on Digital Surveillance
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court delivered a significant judgment reinforcing the fundamental right to data privacy, ruling against indiscriminate digital surveillance by state agencies.
• The verdict emphasized the need for a robust legal framework, proportionality, and judicial oversight for any surveillance activities, even in matters of national security.
• It called for a review of existing surveillance laws and guidelines to align them with constitutional principles, particularly Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty).
• The ruling is expected to have far-reaching implications for government data collection policies, tech companies operating in India, and individual digital rights.
• Experts laud the judgment as a crucial step towards strengthening democratic freedoms and safeguarding citizens' privacy in the digital age.

• Recommendations include simultaneous elections (Lok Sabha and State Assemblies), state funding of elections, and stricter regulations on political donations.
• The panel also suggested measures to curb the criminalization of politics, enhance the transparency of electoral bonds, and improve voter registration processes.
• Emphasis was placed on leveraging technology for efficient election management and ensuring greater participation of women and marginalized sections.
• The report aims to address long-standing issues of electoral malpractices and strengthen democratic principles in India.

GS2.trivia: The Indus Waters Treaty is one of the most successful water-sharing treaties in the world, having survived multiple conflicts between India and Pakistan.

GS3.headline: India Unveils Phase II of National Green Hydrogen Mission, Targets Global Leadership
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) launched Phase II of the National Green Hydrogen Mission (NGHM), focusing on scaling up production and establishing export hubs.
• This phase emphasizes R&D in electrolysis technologies, development of green hydrogen derivatives (e.g., green ammonia), and creation of a robust supply chain.
• Financial incentives and viability gap funding for early-stage projects are key components, alongside skill development programs for a green hydrogen workforce.
• The mission aims to reduce dependence on fossil fuel imports, create significant employment opportunities, and position India as a global leader in green hydrogen production and export.
• Environmental impact assessments and sustainable water management practices are integrated to ensure ecological balance.

Environmental pollution and degradation
Conservation

Key objectives include reducing fossil fuel imports and creating jobs.

mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of green hydrogen for India's energy security and climate goals.

Evaluate the potential of NGHM Phase II to transform India into a global green hydrogen hub.
Examine the socio-economic and environmental implications of a large-scale green hydrogen economy in India.
GS3.imageDescription: Illustration of a green hydrogen plant with wind turbines and solar panels in the background.
GS3.trivia: Hydrogen is the most abundant chemical substance in the universe, making up roughly 75% of all baryonic mass.

GS3.headline: RBI Expands Digital Rupee (CBDC) Pilot Program to Include More Retail Users and Use Cases
GS3.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) announced a significant expansion of its Central Bank Digital Currency (CBDC) pilot project, 'e₹-R' (retail digital rupee).
• The expansion aims to onboard a larger number of retail users and small businesses, testing various new use cases beyond basic peer-to-peer and peer-to-merchant transactions.
• Focus areas include offline functionality, programmable payments, and integration with existing UPI infrastructure to enhance interoperability.
• The RBI seeks to gather more comprehensive data on user experience, technological robustness, and economic impact before a full-scale rollout.
• This move signals India's continued commitment to exploring the potential of digital currencies for financial inclusion and efficient payments.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
prelimsPoints: RBI launched wholesale (e₹-W) and retail (e₹-R) CBDC pilots.
CBDC is a legal tender issued by a central bank in digital form.
It aims to complement, not replace, existing payment systems.
UPI (Unified Payments Interface) is a real-time payment system developed by NPCI.
mainsPoints: Discuss the potential benefits and risks associated with the introduction of a Central Bank Digital Currency (CBDC) in India.
Analyze how the expansion of the digital rupee pilot can impact financial inclusion, monetary policy, and the banking sector.
Examine the technological and regulatory challenges in implementing a successful CBDC in a diverse economy like India.
Compare and contrast CBDC with cryptocurrencies and traditional digital payment methods like UPI.
GS3.imageDescription: Digital representation of the Indian Rupee symbol with binary code background.
GS3.trivia: Globally, over 100 countries are exploring or have launched their own CBDCs, with The Bahamas being one of the first to issue a retail CBDC (the Sand Dollar).

GS3.headline: ISRO Gears Up for Chandrayaan-4 Mission, Eyes Lunar Sample Return
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) announced accelerated preparations for its ambitious Chandrayaan-4 mission, targeting a lunar sample return.
• This mission aims to collect lunar soil and rock samples from a specific region of the Moon and bring them back to Earth for detailed scientific analysis.
• Chandrayaan-4 will involve a complex multi-module architecture, including an orbiter, lander, ascender, and re-entry module, showcasing advanced Indian space capabilities.
• The mission holds significant scientific promise, potentially shedding new light on the Moon's geological history, composition, and evolution.
• This follows the successful Chandrayaan-3 landing, further cementing India's position in lunar exploration and deep space missions.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
prelimsPoints: Chandrayaan-1 was India's first lunar probe.
Chandrayaan-3 successfully soft-landed on the Moon's South Pole.
ISRO is the primary space agency of India.
Sample return missions are highly complex and provide invaluable scientific data.
mainsPoints: Discuss the scientific significance and technological challenges of lunar sample return missions.
Analyze how India's lunar missions (Chandrayaan series) contribute to global space exploration efforts and national prestige.
Examine the role of ISRO in fostering scientific temper and technological advancements in India.
Evaluate the potential socio-economic benefits derived from advancements in space technology.
GS3.imageDescription: Artistic rendering of Chandrayaan-4 modules on the lunar surface, collecting samples.
GS3.trivia: The Moon's South Pole is of particular interest to scientists due to the potential presence of water ice in permanently shadowed craters.

GS3.headline: Government Fast-Tracks Multi-Modal Connectivity Projects in Northeast India
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Development of North Eastern Region (DoNER) announced accelerated implementation of several multi-modal connectivity projects.
• Projects include new railway lines, expansion of national highways, inland waterways development, and upgradation of regional airports.
• The aim is to enhance economic integration of the Northeast with mainland India and neighboring countries, particularly under the 'Act East' policy.
• These initiatives are expected to boost trade, tourism, and investment, unlocking the region's vast potential and reducing geographical isolation.
• Special focus is on sustainable infrastructure development, respecting the region's ecological fragility and unique cultural heritage.

Bharatmala Pariyojana and Sagarmala Pariyojana are major infrastructure initiatives.
Inland Waterways Authority of India (IWAI) develops waterways.

Examine how multi-modal connectivity can foster inclusive growth and address socio-economic disparities in the region.
GS3.imageDescription: Map of Northeast India showing various transport routes converging.
GS3.trivia: The Brahmaputra River, a key inland waterway in the Northeast, is one of the largest rivers in the world by discharge.

GS3.headline: Government Intensifies Efforts to Promote Millets Cultivation and Consumption
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare launched new initiatives to further promote the cultivation and consumption of millets, declared as 'Shree Anna'.
• Efforts include increased Minimum Support Price (MSP) for millets, research into high-yielding and climate-resilient varieties, and awareness campaigns.
• The government aims to position millets as a 'superfood' due to their nutritional benefits and low water footprint, aligning with sustainable agriculture goals.
• Support is being provided to farmers for value addition, processing, and market linkages to enhance their income from millet farming.
• This push comes in the wake of the International Year of Millets (2023) and India's leadership in promoting these ancient grains globally.

Evaluate the potential of millets to contribute to global food security and sustainable diets.
GS3.imageDescription: Various types of millets grains displayed in bowls.
GS3.trivia: Millets were among the first crops domesticated by humans, with evidence of their cultivation dating back over 7,000 years.

GS3.headline: India Strengthens Cybersecurity Framework for Critical Infrastructure
GS3.explainer:
• The National Critical Information Infrastructure Protection Centre (NCIIPC) unveiled an updated cybersecurity framework for critical sectors.
• The new guidelines mandate enhanced threat detection systems, regular security audits, and strict data protection protocols for organizations managing essential services.
• Focus areas include energy grids, financial systems, transportation networks, and communication infrastructure, which are vulnerable to cyber-attacks.
• The move comes amidst rising global cyber threats and aims to safeguard national security and economic stability.
• Collaboration between government agencies, private sector entities, and international partners is emphasized for a resilient cyber ecosystem.
gsSubTopics: Challenges to internal security through communication networks, role of media and social networking sites in internal security challenges, basics of cyber security; money-laundering and its prevention.
Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.

Critical Information Infrastructure (CII) is assets vital to national security and economy.
CERT-In (Indian Computer Emergency Response Team) is the national agency for cyber security incidents.
Cybersecurity is crucial for digital economy and national defense.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolving nature of cyber threats and their implications for India's critical infrastructure and national security.

Examine the challenges in securing critical information infrastructure from sophisticated cyber-attacks.
Suggest measures for fostering a collaborative approach between government, industry, and academia to build a robust cyber defense mechanism.
GS3.imageDescription: Digital lock icon with lines connecting various infrastructure symbols.
GS3.trivia: The first computer virus, 'Elk Cloner', appeared in 1982, affecting Apple II systems.

GS3.headline: National Plan for Wetland Conservation Launches with Focus on Biodiversity and Livelihoods
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) unveiled a comprehensive National Plan for Wetland Conservation and Management.
• The plan emphasizes protection of wetlands as critical ecosystems, crucial for biodiversity, water purification, and climate regulation.
• It integrates local community participation in conservation efforts, promoting sustainable livelihoods dependent on wetlands.
• Key strategies include mapping and inventory of wetlands, restoration of degraded sites, and strict regulation of activities impacting wetland health.
• The initiative aligns with India's commitments under the Ramsar Convention and aims to enhance ecological security and climate resilience.

Biodiversity and its conservation.
prelimsPoints: India is a signatory to the Ramsar Convention on Wetlands.
Ramsar sites are wetlands of international importance.

MoEFCC is the nodal ministry for environmental protection in India.

GS3.headline: Start-up India Seed Fund Scheme Shows Promising Impact on Emerging Ventures
GS3.explainer:
• The Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT) released an impact report on the Start-up India Seed Fund Scheme (SISFS).
• The report indicates a significant boost to early-stage startups, enabling them to develop prototypes, conduct market entry, and commercialize products.
• SISFS has particularly benefited startups in Tier 2 and Tier 3 cities, fostering innovation and entrepreneurship beyond major metropolitan areas.
• Challenges remain in scaling up funding for later stages and ensuring mentorship for diverse sectors.
• The scheme, launched in 2021, aims to provide financial assistance to eligible startups for proof of concept, prototype development, product trials, market entry, and commercialization.

GS3.headline: Indian Antarctic Expedition Concludes, Yields Critical Climate Change Data
GS3.explainer:
• The latest Indian scientific expedition to Antarctica successfully concluded, with researchers returning with vital data on climate change.
• Studies focused on glacial melt rates, sea-level rise, atmospheric composition, and Antarctic biodiversity, contributing to global climate models.
• The expedition utilized India's permanent research stations, 'Bharati' and 'Maitri', for long-term observations and sample collection.
• The findings are expected to enhance understanding of polar science and the impacts of global warming on the Indian subcontinent's weather patterns.
• India reiterated its commitment to peaceful scientific research in Antarctica under the Antarctic Treaty System.

The National Centre for Polar and Ocean Research (NCPOR) manages India's polar programs.

Research focuses on climate change, ozone depletion, and earth sciences.

GS3.trivia: India launched its first scientific expedition to Antarctica in 1981, marking its entry into polar research.
